    1. get()

Interface ConnectionSupplier

Functional Interface:
This is a functional interface and can therefore be used as the assignment target for a lambda expression or method reference.
@FunctionalInterface public interface ConnectionSupplier
Provides the JDBC Connection that should participate in the caller's transaction.

Contract

JdbcOutbox obtains a connection from this supplier on every publish and publishAll call. The supplier MUST satisfy the following:

  • Same transaction as the caller's business writes. The returned Connection must be the one bound to the surrounding transaction so that the outbox INSERT commits or rolls back atomically with those writes. In Spring this is what DataSourceUtils.getConnection(dataSource) returns; in plain JDBC this is the Connection the caller is currently holding open.
  • Manual transaction mode. The returned connection MUST have autoCommit == false. JdbcOutbox asserts this on every publish and throws OutboxConfigurationException otherwise. An autocommit connection silently breaks the atomicity guarantee — the outbox row would be visible to the relay before the business row, defeating the pattern.
  • Open and usable. The supplier returns a non-null, open connection. If obtaining the connection fails (pool exhaustion, network error, …), the supplier throws SQLException and JdbcOutbox translates it.
  • Lifecycle owned by the caller. JdbcOutbox never calls close(), commit(), or rollback() on the returned connection. The Spring transaction manager (or the caller's own try-with-resources) does that.

Examples

Spring (recommended). Inside an @Transactional method, the autoconfiguration in outbox-spring sets the supplier to: 

connectionSupplier(() -> DataSourceUtils.getConnection(dataSource))

Plain JDBC. The caller manages the connection per unit of work and pins it to the supplier for the duration: 

try (Connection connection = dataSource.getConnection()) {
    connection.setAutoCommit(false);
    Outbox outbox =
            JdbcOutbox.builder().connectionSupplier(() -> connection).build();
    try {
        persistBusinessRow(connection);
        outbox.publish(event);
        connection.commit();
    } catch (RuntimeException ex) {
        connection.rollback();
        throw ex;
    }
}

Anti-patterns

  • DO NOT call dataSource.getConnection() from the supplier directly when running under a Spring transaction — the returned connection will be a fresh one outside the active transaction. Use DataSourceUtils.getConnection(dataSource) instead.
  • DO NOT enable autocommit. JdbcOutbox fails fast if it sees one.
  • DO NOT close the returned connection inside the supplier (e.g. by wrapping it in a try-with-resources). The caller still needs the connection to commit the surrounding transaction.
Since:
0.1.0

    Modifier and Type
    Method
    Description
    Connection
    get()
    Returns the Connection bound to the caller's current transaction.

  • Method Details

    • get

      Connection get() throws SQLException
      Returns the Connection bound to the caller's current transaction.
      Returns:
      the active Connection.
      Throws:
      SQLException - if obtaining the connection fails.
      Since:
      0.1.0
